papers.title: Red Runaways: Hypervelocity Stars, Hills Ejecta and Other Outliers in the F-M Star Regime
papers.author_names: John J. Vickers Martin C. Smith Eva K. Grebel

abstract.text: In this paper we analyze a sample of metal-rich (>-0.8 dex) main sequence stars in the extended solar neighborhood, investigating kinematic outliers from the background population. The data, which are taken from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, are kinematically profiled as a function of distance from the Galactic plane using full six dimensional phase space information. Each star is examined in the context of these kinematic profiles and likelihoods are assigned to quantify whether a star matches the underlying profile. Since some of these stars are likely to have been ejected from the disc, we trace back their orbits in order to determine potential ejection radii. We find that objects with low probability (i.e. `outliers') are typically more metal poor, faster and, most importantly, have a tendency to originate from the inner Galaxy compared to the underlying population.\\\n  We also compose a sample of stars with velocities exceeding the local escape velocity. Although we do not discount that our sample could be contaminated by objects with spurious proper motions, a number of stars appear to have been ejected from the disc with exceptionally high velocities. Some of these are consistent with being ejected from the spiral arms and hence are a rich resource for further study. Finally we look at objects whose orbits are consistent with them being ejected at high speeds from the Galactic center. Of these objects we find that one, J135855.65+552538.19, is inconsistent with halo, bulge and disk kinematics and could plausibly have been ejected from the Galactic nucleus via a Hills mechanism.

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] This study analyzes a large sample of metal-rich F-to-M main sequence stars in the solar neighborhood using six-dimensional phase space kinematics from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. It identifies kinematic outliers—stars with high peculiar velocities and low likelihoods of belonging to the background disc population—showing they are more metal-poor, faster, and originate from the inner Galaxy. The paper finds several stars with velocities exceeding the Milky Way’s escape velocity, including one candidate (J135855.65+552538.19) consistent with ejection from the Galactic center via the Hills mechanism, suggesting a significant population of low-mass hypervelocity stars ejected from the disc or central regions.

source_fields.title: Red Runaways: Hypervelocity Stars, Hills Ejecta and Other Outliers in the F-M Star Regime

source_fields.sections_text: 2. Data: This section describes the use of SDSS Data Release 10 spectroscopic data, focusing on high-quality stellar spectra with precise atmospheric parameters, radial velocities, and proper motions to identify disk-origin objects with non-disk kinematics, while excluding halo members based on metallicity criteria. 4. Analysis: This section analyzes outliers in stellar kinematics, showing that they are earlier-type, metal-poor, fast-moving stars with ejection radii closer to the Galactic center, contrasting with natural stars that are later-type, metal-rich, slowly rotating, and orbiting near the Sun's position. 5. Conclusion: This study identifies kinematic outliers and hypervelocity stars in high-metallicity F-to-M stars, finding they are faster, more metal-poor, and centrally concentrated, with binary interactions and spiral arm dynamics providing key explanations for their motions, while some infalling stars suggest potential interactions with the galactic center. 1. Introduction: This paper advances the understanding of Galactic halo runaway stars by leveraging large-scale spectroscopic surveys to identify and characterize low-mass, faint runaway stars—previously overlooked—thereby testing and refining binary and dynamical ejection theories beyond high-mass early-type stars. 6. Acknowledgments: This section acknowledges the contributors, software tools, funding agencies, and collaborative institutions that supported the research. 3. Fitting and Likelihoods: Kinematic profiles of stars are fitted using Gaussian mixture models across different galactic heights, with separate disk and halo components accounting for non-Gaussian rotation and asymmetric drift, and parameters estimated via maximum likelihood using Markov Chain Monte Carlo. Appendix A Appendix A: Assessment of Proper Motion Accuracy: The reliability of SDSS proper motion measurements is assessed by comparing multiple catalogs and finding that agreement is best when SDSS and PPMXL motions are similar, errors are small, and the sky is uncrowded.

title: Red Runaways: Hypervelocity Stars, Hills Ejecta and Other Outliers in the F-M Star Regime

catalog_assessment.evidence: Analyzes a sample of metal-rich main sequence stars from SDSS using full 6D phase space information, identifies kinematic outliers, and specifically discusses candidate J135855.65+552538.19 as a potential Hills ejecta.

papers.title: 19 low mass hyper-velocity star candidates from the first data release of LAMOST survey
papers.author_names: Yin-bi Li A-Li Luo Gang Zhao You-jun Lu Peng Wei Bing Du Xiang Li Yong-Heng Zhao Zhan-wen Han Bo Wang Yue Wu Yong Zhang Yong-hui Hou Yue-fei Wang Ming Yang

abstract.text: Hyper-velocity stars are believed to be ejected out from the Galactic center through dynamical interactions between (binary) stars and the central massive black hole(s). In this paper, we report 19 low mass F/G/K type hyper-velocity star candidates from over one mil- lion stars of the first data release of the LAMOST general survey. We determine the unbound probability for each candidate using a Monte-Carlo simulation by assuming a non-Gaussian proper-motion error distribution, Gaussian heliocentric distance and radial velocity error dis- tributions. The simulation results show that all the candidates have unbound possibilities over 50% as expected, and one of them may even exceed escape velocity with over 90% probabili- ty. In addition, we compare the metallicities of our candidates with the metallicity distribution functions of the Galactic bulge, disk, halo and globular cluster, and conclude that the Galactic bulge or disk is likely the birth place for our candidates.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper identifies 19 low-mass F/G/K-type hyper-velocity star candidates from LAMOST DR1 data using a combination of spectroscopic, photometric, and kinematic criteria. Unbound probabilities are computed via Monte-Carlo simulations accounting for non-Gaussian proper-motion errors, with all candidates showing >50% unbound likelihood and one exceeding escape velocity with >90% probability. Metallicities of the candidates align with Galactic bulge or disk origins, suggesting a disk or bulge birthplace for these stars, challenging the traditional halo origin assumption and supporting a top-heavy initial mass function in the parent population.

source_fields.title: 19 low mass hyper-velocity star candidates from the first data release of LAMOST survey

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a Monte-Carlo simulation method that models proper-motion and radial velocity errors using non-Gaussian and Gaussian distributions, respectively, to estimate the unbound probability of each high-velocity star candidate by comparing their total space velocities to Galactic escape velocities in a million random realizations. Discussion: The study identifies 19 hyper-velocity star candidates from LAMOST data, all with unbound probabilities exceeding 50% (one over 90%), suggesting they likely escaped the Milky Way, with metallicities indicating probable origins in the Galactic bulge or disk rather than the halo or globular clusters, and highlights the need for high-resolution spectroscopy and proper-motion confirmation from HST or GAIA to verify their status and binary nature. Introduction: This paper addresses the gap in identifying low-mass, metal-poor hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by leveraging the LAMOST survey to discover and characterize solar-mass HVSs, particularly those from the white dwarf + helium star scenario, thereby providing critical observational evidence for alternative ejection mechanisms beyond galactic center binary disruptions. Metallicity Distribution And Possible Origins: The metallicity distributions of different Galactic populations reveal distinct chemical abundances, allowing astronomers to infer the likely origins of high-velocity star candidates by comparing their [Fe/H] values to known metallicity distributions of the bulge, disk, halo, and globular clusters. Identification Of The Lamost Dr1 Hvs Candidates: This section describes a five-step process to identify halo variable stars (HVS) candidates from LAMOST DR1 F, G, and K dwarfs by filtering based on spectral type, photometry, extinction, proper motion, and distance estimates using photometric parallaxes. The First Data Release Of The Lamost General Survey: The first data release of the LAMOST General Survey (DR1) includes 2.2 million wavelength-calibrated spectra of stars, galaxies, quasars, and unknown objects, covering a wide wavelength range with high resolution and providing essential spectroscopic parameters for studying stellar populations and galactic structure. low mass hyper-velocity star candidates from the first data release of LAMOST survey: This study identifies 19 low-mass hyper-velocity star candidates from the LAMOST survey, showing high unbinding probabilities and suggesting they likely originated in the Galactic bulge or disk.

abstract: Hyper-velocity stars are believed to be ejected out from the Galactic center through dynamical interactions between (binary) stars and the central massive black hole(s).
In this paper, we report 19 low mass F/G/K type hyper-velocity star candidates from over one mil- lion stars of the first data release of the LAMOST general survey.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title explicitly states '19 low mass hyper-velocity star candidates from LAMOST DR1'. Paper reports specific candidates, computes unbound probabilities, and analyzes their metallicities.

papers.title: MOCCA Code for Star Cluster Simulations - IV. A new Scenario for Intermediate Mass Black Hole Formation in Globular Clusters
papers.author_names: Mirek Giersz Nathan Leigh Arkadiusz Hypki Nora Lützgendorf Abbas Askar

abstract.text: We discuss a new scenario for the formation of intermediate mass black holes in dense star clusters. In this scenario, intermediate mass black holes are formed as a result of dynamical interactions of hard binaries containing a stellar mass black hole, with other stars and binaries. We discuss the necessary conditions to initiate the process of intermediate mass black hole formation and the influence of an intermediate mass black hole on the host global globular cluster properties. We discuss two scenarios for intermediate mass black hole formation. The SLOW and FAST scenarios. They occur later or earlier in the cluster evolution and require smaller or extremely large central densities, respectively. In our simulations, the formation of intermediate mass black holes is highly stochastic. In general, higher formation probabilities follow from larger cluster concentrations (i.e. central densities). We further discuss possible observational signatures of the presence of intermediate mass black holes in globular clusters that follow from our simulations. These include the spatial and kinematic structure of the host cluster, possible radio, X-ray and gravitational wave emissions due to dynamical collisions or mass-transfer and the creation of hypervelocity main sequence escapers during strong dynamical interactions between binaries and an intermediate mass black hole. All simulations discussed in this paper were performed with the MOCCA Monte Carlo code. MOCCA accurately follows most of the important physical processes that occur during the dynamical evolution of star clusters but, as with other dynamical codes, it approximates the dissipative processes connected with stellar collisions and binary mergers.

triage.reason: Weak title match: stellar interactions in dense systems LLM confirmed: Abstract explicitly mentions creation of hypervelocity main sequence escapers during dynamical interactions with an intermediate mass black hole

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes MOCCA, a Monte Carlo cluster simulator that models star cluster evolution through spherical symmetry and two-body relaxation, using a time step proportional to the local relaxation time to efficiently track dynamical processes while incorporating stellar and binary evolution, small-N scattering, and realistic escape mechanisms. Results: The experiment reveals two distinct regimes of black hole mass growth— a rapid "FAST" scenario initiated early after stellar formation and a slower "SLOW" scenario beginning near core collapse— with key performance metrics showing significant BH mass buildup over evolutionary timescales up to 80 Gyr, highlighting the role of dynamical collisions and mergers in IMBH formation. Conclusions: This section concludes that IMBHs form stochastically in dense star clusters via binary dynamical interactions and collisions, with growth occurring in two regimes—slow at moderate densities or fast at extreme densities—and are primarily observable through their indirect effects on cluster structure and compact object populations. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel theoretical framework that addresses the formation of intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) in globular clusters by reconciling observational constraints on GC dynamics and stellar populations with realistic binary evolution and runaway merger scenarios, thereby solving the long-standing problem of IMBH formation without relying on unverified extreme initial conditions like top-heavy initial mass functions. Initial Conditions: This section describes the initial conditions of 400 star cluster simulations designed to explore binary fraction, cluster concentration, and mass function correlations, with a focus on modeling primordial binaries and their impact on intermediate-mass black hole formation. Galactic Globular Clusters: This section explores whether models with intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) formation occupy distinct regions in escape velocity–binary interaction probability space compared to models without IMBH formation, finding that IMBH formation is more likely in systems with high binary interaction probabilities and low escape velocities, particularly in reduced mass accretion scenarios. Observational Signatures of IMBHs in Globular Clusters: Observational signatures of intermediate-mass black holes in globular clusters include shallow surface brightness profiles with large core radii and centrally rising velocity dispersion profiles, though these signals can be obscured by bright stars or become dominated by the black hole's mass at late times.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper about IMBH formation scenarios in globular clusters using the MOCCA code. Mentions hypervelocity escapers as a possible signature but presents no observational data or object-level catalog.
